questions.question: Which is the highest court in India?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India is the apex court and the final interpreter of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which article provides for the establishment of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 of the Constitution establishes the Supreme Court of India.

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Review' in India means:
options: The judiciary can declare laws unconstitutional
questions.explanation: Judicial Review gives the Supreme Court and High Courts the power to examine legislation and executive actions and declare them void if they violate the Constitution.

questions.question: The Supreme Court of India was established on:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India was established on 28 January 1950. The Federal Court of India (1937–1950) was its predecessor. The first Chief Justice was H.J. Kania.

questions.question: The principle of 'Judicial Review' allows courts to:
options: Examine and strike down laws/executive actions that violate the Constitution
questions.explanation: Judicial Review (borrowed from the USA) allows Indian courts (especially SC and HCs) to examine the constitutional validity of legislation and executive actions, and strike them down if they violate the Constitution. Articles 13, 32, 226 provide for this.

questions.question: The original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court is laid down in:
questions.explanation: Article 131: Original jurisdiction of SC — disputes between states, or between Centre and states. Article 132-134: Appellate jurisdiction. Article 136: Special Leave Petition (SLP).

questions.question: The Collegium system for appointment of judges was established in:
questions.explanation: The Collegium system emerged from three Supreme Court cases: First Judges Case (1982), Second Judges Case (1993) — established collegium, Third Judges Case (1998) — clarified composition.

questions.question: The National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC) was struck down in:
questions.explanation: The NJAC (99th Constitutional Amendment, 2014) was struck down by the Supreme Court in 2015 (Fourth Judges Case) as unconstitutional — violating judicial independence (Basic Structure).

questions.question: Which Union Territory has its own Legislature and High Court?
questions.explanation: Delhi (National Capital Territory) and Puducherry are the only UTs with their own Legislative Assemblies (and thus elected governments). J&K is now a UT with Legislature after 2019.

questions.question: The original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court (Art 131) covers disputes between:
options: Government of India and one or more States
questions.explanation: Article 131 gives the SC exclusive original jurisdiction in disputes between: (a) GoI and one or more States; (b) GoI and States on one side and States on other; (c) Two or more States.

questions.question: 'Curative Petition' in the Supreme Court was recognized in which case?
options: Rupa Ashok Hurra v. Ashok Hurra (2002)
questions.explanation: The concept of a Curative Petition was established in Rupa Ashok Hurra v. Ashok Hurra (2002). It is the last constitutional remedy after the SC dismisses a review petition.

questions.question: The 1st Constitutional Amendment (1951) was mainly related to:
options: Adding 9th Schedule to protect land reform laws
questions.explanation: The 1st Amendment (1951) added the 9th Schedule to protect land reform laws from judicial review, added Art 15(4) for OBC/SC/ST reservations, and restricted right to property and trade.

questions.question: The Madras High Court was established in:
questions.explanation: The Madras High Court was established on 15 August 1862 under the High Courts Act, 1861. It is one of the three Presidency High Courts established by the British (others: Bombay, Calcutta).

questions.question: High Court judges retire at the age of:
questions.explanation: High Court judges (Art 217) retire at 62 years of age. Supreme Court judges (Art 124) retire at 65 years. This is different from the 7th Pay Commission recommendation of 65 for HCs.

questions.question: Who appoints the Advocate General of a State?
questions.explanation: The Advocate General is appointed by the Governor (Art 165). He/she must be qualified to be a judge of the High Court and holds office during the pleasure of the Governor.

questions.question: The ADM Jabalpur case (1976) was overruled by the Supreme Court in:
questions.explanation: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla (1976) — where the SC infamously held that right to life can be suspended during Emergency — was explicitly overruled by the 9-judge bench in K.S. Puttaswamy (2017).

questions.question: 'NOTA' (None of the Above) option was introduced in Indian elections in:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court in PUCL v. Union of India (2013) directed the Election Commission to provide NOTA option. NOTA was first used in 5 State Assembly elections in November 2013.

questions.question: Under Article 239AA, the NCT of Delhi's legislature cannot make laws on:
questions.explanation: Art 239AA (inserted by 69th Amendment, 1991) gives Delhi's legislature power on all matters EXCEPT three: Police, Public Order, and Land. These remain with the Central Government. This was upheld by the Supreme Court.

questions.question: The Special Leave Petition (SLP) in the Supreme Court is filed under:
questions.explanation: Article 136 empowers the Supreme Court to grant special leave to appeal from any judgment or order of any court or tribunal (except military tribunals). This is a discretionary power.

questions.question: How many High Courts are there in India currently?
questions.explanation: India currently has 25 High Courts. The latest ones established include the High Court for Telangana (2019) and High Courts for Jammu, Srinagar (after J&K reorganization). Some HCs have benches in multiple cities.

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Activism' in India refers to:
options: Courts actively using their power to protect rights and check government excesses
Courts acting faster in disposing cases
questions.explanation: Judicial Activism refers to the tendency of judges to actively protect citizens' rights through expansive interpretations of constitutional provisions, PILs, and suo motu actions — going beyond traditional passive adjudication.

questions.question: India's Constitution is described as 'federal in form but unitary in spirit' because:
options: It has a strong Centre with residual powers and emergency provisions to override States
questions.explanation: India's Constitution has federal features (division of powers, bicameral legislature, independent judiciary) but unitary features dominate: single citizenship, integrated judiciary, strong Centre, emergency provisions that can override States.

questions.question: Which article deals with the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court in disputes between the Centre and States?
questions.explanation: Article 131 confers original jurisdiction on the Supreme Court in disputes between the Government of India and one or more States, or between two or more States.

questions.question: The 'Basic Structure' doctrine was evolved by the Supreme Court in:
questions.explanation: The Basic Structure doctrine was established in Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) — a 13-judge bench decision by a 7:6 majority.

questions.question: Which High Court has jurisdiction over Tamil Nadu?
questions.explanation: The Madras High Court has jurisdiction over Tamil Nadu and the Union Territory of Puducherry. Established in 1862, it is one of the oldest High Courts in India.

questions.question: The retirement age of a Supreme Court judge is:
questions.explanation: Supreme Court judges retire at 65 years (Article 124). High Court judges retire at 62 years. This was the only age limit originally — fixed at 65 by the Constitution.

questions.question: 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) was developed by:
questions.explanation: PIL was developed by the Supreme Court of India in the 1980s, especially through Justices V.R. Krishna Iyer and P.N. Bhagwati, allowing any citizen to approach courts for public interest.

questions.question: Who is the custodian of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India is the custodian and final interpreter of the Constitution. It has the power of judicial review to strike down any law or executive action that violates the Constitution.

questions.question: The tenure of the Chief Election Commissioner is:
options: 6 years or till age 65, whichever is earlier
questions.explanation: The Chief Election Commissioner holds office for 6 years or until age 65, whichever is earlier. The same applies to Election Commissioners. They can be removed only like a Supreme Court judge.

questions.question: The Indra Sawhney case (1992) ruled that total reservation cannot exceed:
questions.explanation: In Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (Mandal case, 1992), the Supreme Court upheld OBC reservation but set the ceiling at 50% for all reservations combined (with exceptions for extraordinary situations).

questions.question: The three organs of the Indian Government are:
options: Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha, Supreme Court
questions.explanation: The three organs of government are: Legislature (makes laws), Executive (implements laws), and Judiciary (interprets laws).

questions.question: NOTA (None of the Above) option on EVMs was introduced in:
questions.explanation: NOTA was introduced following a Supreme Court order (PUCL case, 2013). Voters can press the NOTA button to reject all candidates while exercising their right to vote.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the establishment of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 establishes the Supreme Court of India.

questions.question: Who appoints the judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts?
questions.explanation: The President of India appoints judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts under Articles 124 and 217.

questions.question: What is the minimum age required for a person to be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: The Constitution does not prescribe any minimum age for appointment as a Supreme Court judge.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to declare a law unconstitutional is called:
questions.explanation: Judicial review is the power to declare laws or executive actions unconstitutional.

questions.question: Under which Article can the President of India seek the opinion of the Supreme Court on a question of law or fact?
questions.explanation: Article 143 empowers the President to consult the Supreme Court for advisory opinion.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued to prevent a subordinate court from exceeding its jurisdiction?
questions.explanation: Writ of Prohibition is issued by a higher court to prevent a lower court from acting beyond its jurisdiction.

questions.question: A judge of the Supreme Court can be removed from office on the ground of proved misbehaviour or incapacity by a motion passed by:
questions.explanation: Removal requires a special majority in both Houses of Parliament under Article 124(4).

questions.question: The concept of 'basic structure' of the Constitution was propounded by the Supreme Court in which landmark case?
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine was established in the Kesavananda Bharati (1973) case.

questions.question: Which High Court in India was the first to be established?
questions.explanation: The Calcutta High Court was established in 1862 as the first High Court in India.

questions.question: The term 'judicial review' in the Indian Constitution is considered part of:
questions.explanation: Judicial review is a part of the basic structure of the Constitution and cannot be abrogated.

questions.question: What is the retirement age of a judge of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: Article 124(2) of the Constitution sets the retirement age of Supreme Court judges at 65 years.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution grants the Supreme Court the power of judicial review?
questions.explanation: Article 13 declares any law inconsistent with Fundamental Rights as void, forming the basis of judicial review.

questions.question: What is the current sanctioned strength of judges in the Supreme Court of India (including the Chief Justice)?
questions.explanation: As per the Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Act, 2019, the sanctioned strength is 34 judges.

questions.question: Who appoints the judges of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: The President appoints Supreme Court judges in consultation with the Chief Justice and other judges as per Article 124.

questions.question: What is the procedure for the removal of a Supreme Court judge?
options: By a majority in Lok Sabha only
By an order of the President
By impeachment in both Houses with special majority
By a resolution of the Cabinet
questions.explanation: A Supreme Court judge can be removed only by impeachment passed by both Houses with a two-thirds majority.

questions.question: Which of the following cases falls under the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 131 gives the Supreme Court original jurisdiction in disputes between the Government of India and states or between states.

questions.question: Under which Article can a citizen directly approach the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 32 guarantees the right to move the Supreme Court for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: Which court is the highest court of appeal in India?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court is the apex court and the final court of appeal under Article 136.

questions.question: The 'Basic Structure' doctrine of the Indian Constitution was established in which landmark case?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
questions.explanation: In 1973, the Kesavananda Bharati case held that the basic structure of the Constitution cannot be amended.

questions.question: Which Article empowers High Courts to issue writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights and other legal rights?
questions.explanation: Article 226 gives High Courts the power to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights and for any other purpose.

questions.question: What is the minimum age for appointment as a judge of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: A person must have attained the age of 55 years to be appointed as a Supreme Court judge.

questions.question: The power of judicial review in India is derived from which principle?
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine, established in the Kesavananda Bharati case, limits Parliament's power to amend core constitutional features.

questions.question: Who is the head of the High Court in a state?
questions.explanation: Each High Court is headed by a Chief Justice, who is appointed by the President.

questions.question: What is the maximum retirement age for a judge of a High Court in India?
questions.explanation: A High Court judge retires at the age of 62 years, while a Supreme Court judge retires at 65.

questions.question: Which court has the power to issue writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Both the Supreme Court (under Article 32) and High Courts (under Article 226) can issue writs for Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: The concept of 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) was introduced in India by which court?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court pioneered PIL in the 1980s to allow any citizen to seek justice on matters of public concern.

questions.question: Which amendment act introduced the provision of the National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC)?
questions.explanation: The 99th Amendment Act (2014) sought to replace the collegium system with NJAC, but it was struck down by the Supreme Court in 2015.

questions.question: Who is the head of the Indian Judiciary?
questions.explanation: The Chief Justice of India is the head of the Indian Judiciary, presiding over the Supreme Court.

questions.question: What is the age of retirement for a Judge of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: A Supreme Court judge retires at the age of 65 years as per Article 124 of the Constitution.

questions.question: The power of judicial review in India is derived from which of the following?
questions.explanation: Article 13 declares laws inconsistent with fundamental rights as void, enabling judicial review.

questions.question: The High Court of Tamil Nadu is located in which city?
questions.explanation: The Madras High Court (High Court of Tamil Nadu) is located in Chennai.

questions.question: Under which article can a person move the Supreme Court directly for enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 32 guarantees the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

questions.question: Which type of jurisdiction does the Supreme Court have in disputes between the Centre and States?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in Centre-State disputes under Article 131.

questions.question: The concept of 'Separation of Powers' in the Indian Constitution is borrowed from which country?
questions.explanation: The separation of powers concept in India is inspired by the US Constitution, though not rigidly followed.

questions.question: What is the minimum age required to become a judge of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: The Constitution does not prescribe a minimum age for Supreme Court judges.

questions.question: Which writ is issued to secure the release of a person unlawfully detained?
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus is used to challenge unlawful detention.

questions.question: The power of judicial review in India is derived from which concept?
questions.explanation: Judicial review is based on the basic structure doctrine evolved by the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Who appoints the Chief Justice of a High Court?
questions.explanation: The President appoints the Chief Justice of a High Court after consultation with the Chief Justice of India and the Governor.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution provides for the Supreme Court as the guardian of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 32 confers the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of fundamental rights, making it the guardian.

questions.question: How many judges are there in the original composition of the Supreme Court (excluding the Chief Justice)?
questions.explanation: Initially in 1950, the Supreme Court had 7 judges (including Chief Justice).

questions.question: Which High Court has jurisdiction over the Union Territory of Puducherry?
questions.explanation: The Madras High Court has jurisdiction over Puducherry.

questions.question: The term 'court of record' means the court can record its own proceedings. Which Indian courts are courts of record?
options: Both Supreme Court and High Courts
questions.explanation: Both the Supreme Court and High Courts are courts of record under Articles 129 and 215.

questions.question: Under which article can the Supreme Court transfer a case from one High Court to another?
questions.explanation: Article 139A empowers the Supreme Court to transfer cases between High Courts.

questions.question: Under which article of the Indian Constitution is the Supreme Court established?
questions.explanation: Article 124 provides for the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court of India.

questions.question: What is the retirement age of a Supreme Court judge?
questions.explanation: A Supreme Court judge retires at the age of 65 years as per Article 124(2).

questions.question: Which writ is issued by a higher court to prevent a lower court from exceeding its jurisdiction?
questions.explanation: Writ of Prohibition is issued to prevent a lower court or tribunal from acting beyond its jurisdiction.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to declare a law unconstitutional is known as:
questions.explanation: Judicial review is the power of courts to examine the constitutionality of legislative and executive actions.

questions.question: What is the retirement age of a High Court judge in India?
questions.explanation: A High Court judge retires at the age of 62 years as per Article 217.

questions.question: Under which article of the Constitution are High Court judges appointed?
questions.explanation: Article 217 deals with the appointment and conditions of office of a High Court judge.

questions.question: The advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court is provided under which article?
questions.explanation: Article 143 empowers the President to seek the Supreme Court's opinion on any question of law or fact.

questions.question: The 'Basic Structure Doctrine' was propounded by the Supreme Court in which landmark case?
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati (1973), the Supreme Court held that the basic structure of the Constitution cannot be amended.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 provides for the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court of India.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court of India to review its own judgments is derived from which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 137 of the Constitution empowers the Supreme Court to review its own judgments or orders, subject to the provisions of any law made by Parliament.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued by a court to a public authority to perform a duty it is legally obligated to perform?
questions.explanation: Mandamus is a writ commanding a public authority to perform a statutory duty it has failed or refused to perform.

questions.question: The concept of 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution was first propounded by the Supreme Court in which landmark case?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
questions.explanation: In the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), the Supreme Court held that Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution.

questions.question: Under the Indian Constitution, the power of judicial review of the Supreme Court is a feature of which of the following?
questions.explanation: Judicial review is considered part of the basic structure of the Constitution and ensures the supremacy of the Constitution.

questions.question: The salaries and allowances of the judges of the High Courts are charged on which fund?
questions.explanation: The salaries and allowances of High Court judges are charged on the Consolidated Fund of the respective state, ensuring their independence.

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution of India deals with the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 provides for the establishment of the Supreme Court of India, consisting of a Chief Justice and other judges.

questions.question: A judge of the Supreme Court can be removed from office only on the ground of proven misbehaviour or incapacity, by an order of the President passed after an address by each House of Parliament. This address must be supported by a majority of the total membership of that House and by a majority of not less than:
options: One-half of those present and voting
Two-thirds of those present and voting
Three-fourths of those present and voting
questions.explanation: The removal requires a special majority: a majority of the total membership of each House and a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting.

questions.question: The jurisdiction of the Supreme Court to settle disputes between the Union and States is called:
questions.explanation: Under Article 131, the Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in disputes between the Union and States or between states.

questions.question: In which of the following cases did the Supreme Court rule that the 'Preamble' is a part of the Constitution?
options: L.I.C. of India v. Consumer Education and Research Centre
questions.explanation: In the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), the Supreme Court held that the Preamble is an integral part of the Constitution.

questions.question: The concept of 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) originated in India from which case?
options: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India
M.C. Mehta v. Union of India
Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India
questions.explanation: The PIL concept was introduced in the S.P. Gupta case (1981), also known as the Judges Transfer case, relaxing the rule of locus standi.

questions.question: Under which of the following provisions of the Constitution can the President seek the advisory opinion of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 143 empowers the President to refer any question of law or fact to the Supreme Court for its opinion.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to punish for its contempt is derived from which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 129 declares the Supreme Court a court of record and gives it the power to punish for contempt of itself.

questions.question: Which of the following High Courts in India has the largest territorial jurisdiction?
questions.explanation: The Bombay High Court has jurisdiction over Maharashtra, Goa, Dadra and Nagar Haveli, and Daman and Diu.

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Review' in India is borrowed from the Constitution of which country?
questions.explanation: The power of judicial review, where courts can declare laws unconstitutional, is borrowed from the US Constitution.

questions.question: A Judge of the Supreme Court can be removed from office only on the ground of proved misbehaviour or incapacity, by a resolution passed by:
options: Both Houses of Parliament with a simple majority
Both Houses of Parliament with a special majority
The President on the advice of the Chief Justice
questions.explanation: Removal requires a majority of the total membership of each House and a majority of not less than two-thirds of those present and voting.

questions.question: The 'Basic Structure' doctrine of the Indian Constitution was propounded by the Supreme Court in which landmark case?
questions.explanation: In the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), the Supreme Court held that the basic structure of the Constitution cannot be amended.

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution provides for the establishment of a High Court for each state?
questions.explanation: Article 214 states that there shall be a High Court for each state.

questions.question: The power of the High Court to issue writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights is wider than that of the Supreme Court because:
options: High Court can issue writs for other purposes too
High Court has original jurisdiction in all cases
High Court is not bound by the Supreme Court's decisions
High Court can issue writs against private individuals
questions.explanation: Unlike the Supreme Court, a High Court can issue writs against private individuals and for purposes other than Fundamental Rights enforcement.

questions.question: The 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) mechanism was pioneered by the Indian judiciary primarily to:
options: Provide legal aid to the poor
Protect the rights of the marginalized and enforce public duties
questions.explanation: PIL was developed to allow any public-spirited citizen to approach the court for the enforcement of rights of disadvantaged groups.

questions.question: Under the Indian Constitution, the appellate jurisdiction of the Supreme Court in criminal matters extends to cases where the High Court has:
options: Reversed an order of acquittal and sentenced to death
Convicted a person for a minor offence
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court hears appeals if the High Court reverses an acquittal and awards a death sentence.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution provides for the establishment of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 establishes the Supreme Court of India, while Article 32 deals with the right to constitutional remedies.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to review its own judgment is known as:
questions.explanation: A curative petition is the last judicial remedy available in the Supreme Court to review its own final judgment, even after a review petition is dismissed.

questions.question: Under which provision can the President of India seek the opinion of the Supreme Court on a question of law or fact?
questions.explanation: Article 143 empowers the President to refer any question of law or fact to the Supreme Court for its advisory opinion.

questions.question: Which High Court in India has the largest number of judges?
questions.explanation: The Allahabad High Court has the largest sanctioned strength of judges among all High Courts in India.

questions.question: A Judge of the Supreme Court can be removed from office only on the ground of:
questions.explanation: Article 124(4) states that a Supreme Court judge can be removed only on grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity, by a special majority in Parliament.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution deals with the appointment of District Judges?
questions.explanation: Article 233 governs the appointment of District Judges by the Governor in consultation with the High Court.

questions.question: The 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) was introduced in India to:
options: Provide legal aid to the poor
Enforce fundamental rights of the marginalized
questions.explanation: PIL was innovated by the Supreme Court to enable any public-spirited person to seek enforcement of fundamental rights of disadvantaged groups.

questions.question: The retirement age of a Judge of the Madras High Court is:
questions.explanation: As per Article 217, a High Court judge retires at the age of 62 years, while Supreme Court judges retire at 65.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to review its own judgments is derived from which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 137 empowers the Supreme Court to review its own judgments.

questions.question: Who appoints the Chief Justice of India?
questions.explanation: The President of India appoints the Chief Justice of India under Article 124.

questions.question: What is the minimum age prescribed for appointment as a judge of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: The Constitution does not prescribe a minimum age for Supreme Court judges.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid ground for the removal of a Supreme Court judge?
questions.explanation: Removal is only for proved misbehaviour or incapacity, as per Article 124(4).

questions.question: Under which Article can the Supreme Court issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 32 gives the Supreme Court power to issue writs for Fundamental Rights enforcement.

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Review' in India is borrowed from which country's Constitution?
questions.explanation: Judicial review in India is inspired by the US Constitution.

questions.question: Under which Article does the Supreme Court have the power to transfer cases from one High Court to another?
questions.explanation: Article 139A allows the Supreme Court to transfer cases between High Courts.

questions.question: The concept of 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) in India was propounded by which Supreme Court judge?
questions.explanation: Justice P.N. Bhagwati is credited with liberalizing locus standi and introducing PIL.

questions.question: The power of a High Court to issue writs under Article 226 extends to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Article 226 gives High Courts wider power, covering enforcement of any legal right.

questions.question: The concept of 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) was introduced in India by:
questions.explanation: Justice P.N. Bhagwati and Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer pioneered PIL in the 1980s.

questions.question: Which writ is issued to quash the order of a lower court or tribunal?
questions.explanation: Certiorari is issued to quash the order of a lower court or tribunal when it exceeds jurisdiction.

questions.question: The minimum age for appointment as a judge of the Supreme Court is:
questions.explanation: The Constitution does not prescribe a minimum age for Supreme Court judges; only citizenship and experience requirements.

questions.question: Under which article can the President seek the opinion of the Supreme Court on a question of law or fact?
questions.explanation: Article 143 empowers the President to refer any question of law or fact to the Supreme Court for its opinion.

questions.question: The judges of the Supreme Court can be removed from office only on the ground of:
questions.explanation: Article 124(4) provides for removal by impeachment for proved misbehaviour or incapacity.

questions.question: The doctrine of 'basic structure' of the Constitution was propounded by the Supreme Court in which case?
questions.explanation: In 1973, the Supreme Court in Kesavananda Bharati held that the basic structure of the Constitution cannot be amended.

questions.question: In which of the following cases did the Supreme Court establish the 'basic structure' doctrine?
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine was propounded by the Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), limiting Parliament's amending power.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to review its own judgment is derived from?
questions.explanation: Article 137 of the Constitution empowers the Supreme Court to review its own judgments or orders.

questions.question: Which constitutional amendment introduced the provision for the establishment of a National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC)?
questions.explanation: The 99th Constitutional Amendment Act (2014) sought to replace the collegium system with NJAC, later struck down by the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the appointment of judges of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 provides for the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court and appointment of its judges by the President.

questions.question: The jurisdiction of the High Court to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights is enshrined under which article?
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights and for other purposes, while Article 32 covers the Supreme Court.

questions.question: The 'doctrine of eclipse' under Indian Constitution is associated with?
options: Pre-constitutional laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights
questions.explanation: The doctrine of eclipse holds that pre-constitutional laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights become dormant but can revive if the inconsistency is removed.

questions.question: Which of the following committees recommended the establishment of an All India Judicial Service?
questions.explanation: The Satish Chandra Committee (1999) on judicial reforms recommended the creation of an All India Judicial Service to improve the subordinate judiciary.

questions.question: The 'collegium system' for appointment of judges of higher judiciary was evolved through?
questions.explanation: The collegium system emerged from the three Judges Cases (1981, 1993, 1998) regarding the primacy of the Chief Justice of India in appointments.

questions.question: Which High Court has the largest number of judges (sanctioned strength) in India?
questions.explanation: The Allahabad High Court has the highest sanctioned strength of 160 judges among all High Courts in India.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution provides for the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 provides for the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court of India.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to review its own judgment is derived from which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 137 empowers the Supreme Court to review its own judgments or orders.

questions.question: What is the minimum age required for appointment as a Judge of the High Court?
questions.explanation: A person must have attained the age of 45 years to be appointed as a High Court Judge.

questions.question: The concept of 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) originated in India through which famous case?
options: S.P. Gupta vs Union of India
Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala
Maneka Gandhi vs Union of India
A.K. Gopalan vs State of Madras
questions.explanation: The PIL concept was introduced in the S.P. Gupta vs Union of India case (1981).

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution can the President of India seek the advisory opinion of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 143 allows the President to refer any question of law or fact to the Supreme Court for its opinion.

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution of India provides for the establishment and composition of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 establishes the Supreme Court; the other articles deal with acting judges, additional judges, and appellate jurisdiction.

questions.question: In which landmark case did the Supreme Court of India propound the 'basic structure' doctrine?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
questions.explanation: The Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) established that Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which writ is issued by a court to compel a public authority to perform its statutory duty?
questions.explanation: Mandamus commands a public official to fulfil a duty; other writs serve different purposes like prevention or quashing orders.

questions.question: The Supreme Court's jurisdiction to hear appeals from High Courts in cases involving substantial questions of law is called:
questions.explanation: Appellate jurisdiction allows the SC to review decisions of lower courts; original jurisdiction covers disputes between governments.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT included in the term 'District Judge' as defined in the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Chief Judicial Magistrate is a magisterial post; 'District Judge' includes sessions judges and additional district judges.

questions.question: The Supreme Court's power to issue directions and writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights is conferred by:
questions.explanation: Article 32 guarantees the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights; Article 226 gives similar power to High Courts.

questions.question: Which Article empowers High Courts to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights and other legal rights?
questions.explanation: Article 226 gives High Courts wider writ jurisdiction than the Supreme Court's Article 32, covering legal rights as well.

questions.question: The 'Collegium System' for appointment of judges to the Supreme Court and High Courts was established by:
options: Judicial verdicts of the Supreme Court
An executive order of the President
questions.explanation: The collegium system evolved through Supreme Court judgments, particularly the Three Judges Cases, ensuring judicial independence.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a required qualification for appointment as a judge of the Supreme Court of India?
options: Must be a citizen of India
Must have been a judge of a High Court for at least 5 years
Must have been an advocate of a High Court for at least 10 years
Must have been a District Judge for at least 5 years
questions.explanation: A distinguished jurist can also be appointed; being a District Judge is not a qualification for the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution defines the Supreme Court as a court of record with the power to punish for contempt?
questions.explanation: Article 129 declares the Supreme Court a court of record and confers contempt power.

questions.question: Which provision allows the Supreme Court to grant special leave to appeal from any judgment or order of any court or tribunal in India?
questions.explanation: Article 136 empowers the Supreme Court to grant special leave to appeal.

questions.question: The minimum age for appointment as a judge of the Supreme Court of India is:
questions.explanation: The Constitution does not prescribe a minimum age for Supreme Court judges; only a retirement age of 65.

questions.question: Which of the following High Courts has the largest number of judicial benches?
questions.explanation: Allahabad High Court has the largest sanctioned strength of judges among all High Courts.

questions.question: The power of the High Court to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights is under:
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights and other legal rights.

questions.question: The tenure of a judge of the Supreme Court of India is:
questions.explanation: Supreme Court judges retire at age 65; High Court judges retire at 62.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a qualification for appointment as a judge of the High Court?
options: Must be a citizen of India
Must have been a judge of a district court for at least 5 years
Must have been an advocate of a High Court for at least 10 years
Must have held a judicial office for at least 10 years
questions.explanation: The Constitution requires holding judicial office for 10 years or being an advocate of a High Court for 10 years; not specifically being a district judge for 5 years.

questions.question: In which case did the Supreme Court propound the 'basic structure' doctrine?
options: Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala
Minerva Mills vs Union of India
S.R. Bommai vs Union of India
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine was established by the Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973).

questions.question: The power of judicial review over the decisions of the Election Commission is vested with:
options: Both Supreme Court and High Courts
questions.explanation: Judicial review of Election Commission decisions can be undertaken by both the Supreme Court (Article 32) and High Courts (Article 226).

questions.question: Under which article of the Indian Constitution is the Supreme Court established as the highest court of appeal?
questions.explanation: Article 124 establishes the Supreme Court of India. Article 136 deals with special leave petition, Article 131 with original jurisdiction, and Article 143 with advisory jurisdiction.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to review its own judgment is derived from which source?
questions.explanation: Article 137 empowers the Supreme Court to review its own judgments or orders. Article 32 is for writs, Article 226 for High Court writs, and Article 145 for Supreme Court rules.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued to prevent a lower court or tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction?
questions.explanation: Writ of Prohibition is issued by a higher court to prevent a lower court from acting beyond its jurisdiction. Certiorari quashes an order, Mandamus commands performance, and Quo Warranto challenges authority.

questions.question: What is the minimum age requirement for appointment as a Judge of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: The Constitution does not prescribe any minimum age for appointment as a Supreme Court judge, unlike High Court judges who require 10 years of judicial experience.

questions.question: Which High Court in India has the largest number of judges (sanctioned strength)?
questions.explanation: Allahabad High Court has the highest sanctioned strength of judges (160). Delhi High Court has 60, Bombay 94, and Madras 75.

questions.question: Under Article 226 of the Constitution, a High Court can issue writs for the enforcement of which rights?
options: Fundamental Rights and other legal rights
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights as well as for any other purpose (legal rights). Article 32 is limited to Fundamental Rights only.

questions.question: Who appoints the Chief Justice of a High Court in India?
questions.explanation: The President appoints the Chief Justice of a High Court, after consultation with the Chief Justice of India and the Governor of the state. The Governor does not appoint judges.

questions.question: The 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) mechanism in India was first recognized by the Supreme Court in which case?
options: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India
A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
questions.explanation: The PIL was recognized in S.P. Gupta v. Union of India (1981), also known as the Judges Transfer case. Maneka Gandhi expanded Article 21, and A.K. Gopalan dealt with preventive detention.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution provides for the establishment of a common High Court for two or more states?
questions.explanation: Article 231 allows Parliament to establish a common High Court for two or more states and a Union Territory. Article 214 mandates a High Court for each state, Article 217 deals with appointment, and Article 224 with additional judges.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to review its own judgments is known as:
questions.explanation: A curative petition is the last judicial remedy available in the Supreme Court for review of its own final judgment.

questions.question: The power of judicial review in India is derived from which feature of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Judicial review is primarily used to protect Fundamental Rights, ensuring laws do not violate them.

questions.question: A High Court Judge in India holds office until the age of:
questions.explanation: A High Court Judge retires at the age of 62 years, as per Article 217 of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued to a lower court or tribunal to transfer a case to a higher court?
questions.explanation: Certiorari is issued to quash the order of a lower court and transfer the case to a higher court for review.

questions.question: The concept of 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) in India was pioneered by which Supreme Court judge?
questions.explanation: Justice P.N. Bhagwati is credited with liberalizing locus standi and introducing PIL in the Indian judiciary.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution establishes the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 provides for the establishment of the Supreme Court of India. Other articles deal with related matters like acting Chief Justice or additional judges.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a writ that a High Court can issue under Article 226?
questions.explanation: The five constitutional writs are Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, and Quo Warranto. Injunction is a civil remedy, not a writ.

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Review' in India is borrowed from which country?
questions.explanation: Judicial review is borrowed from the US Constitution, though Indian courts have a wider scope of review.

questions.question: Which amendment to the Indian Constitution added the word 'Secular' to the Preamble?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment (1976) inserted 'Secular' and 'Socialist' into the Preamble.

questions.question: The principal seat of the High Court of Tamil Nadu is located in which city?
questions.explanation: The Madras High Court is situated in Chennai, with a bench at Madurai.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the appointment of High Court judges?
questions.explanation: Article 217 governs the appointment of High Court judges by the President after consultation with the Chief Justice of India and the Governor.

questions.question: The doctrine of 'Basic Structure' was propounded by the Supreme Court in which landmark case?
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973), the Supreme Court established the basic structure doctrine limiting Parliament's amending power.

questions.question: Which writ is issued to compel a public official to perform a statutory duty?
questions.explanation: Mandamus commands a public official to perform a duty. Prohibition prevents lower courts from exceeding jurisdiction, while Quo Warranto challenges usurpation of office.

questions.question: Under the Panchayati Raj system, which body handles dispute resolution at the village level?
questions.explanation: Nyaya Panchayats are informal village-level courts for minor civil and criminal disputes, though not a mandatory part of the constitutional scheme.

questions.question: Who appoints the Chief Justice and other Judges of the High Court in a state?
questions.explanation: The President appoints the Chief Justice and other judges of a High Court after consultation with the Chief Justice of India and the Governor.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution can the President refer a question of law or fact to the Supreme Court for its opinion?
questions.explanation: Article 143 confers advisory jurisdiction on the Supreme Court, allowing the President to seek its opinion.

questions.question: What is the minimum age required for appointment as a Judge of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: The Constitution does not prescribe any minimum age for appointment as a Supreme Court judge.

questions.question: The concept of 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) in India originated from which landmark case?
options: S.P. Gupta vs Union of India
questions.explanation: The S.P. Gupta case (1981) is widely regarded as the origin of Public Interest Litigation in India.

questions.question: The salary and allowances of a High Court judge are charged on which fund?
questions.explanation: The salary and allowances of a High Court judge are charged on the Consolidated Fund of the respective state.

questions.question: Which amendment to the Indian Constitution introduced the 'Doctrine of Basic Structure'?
questions.explanation: The Basic Structure doctrine was propounded by the Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), not by any amendment.

questions.question: In which year was the National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC) Act passed, later struck down by the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: The NJAC Act was passed in 2014 but was declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court in 2015.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of judges (including the Chief Justice) that the Supreme Court of India can have as per the current constitutional provision?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court strength was increased to 34 (including CJI) through the Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Act, 2019.

questions.question: Under which provision of the Constitution can a High Court judge be transferred from one High Court to another?
questions.explanation: Article 222 empowers the President to transfer a High Court judge after consultation with the Chief Justice of India.

questions.question: In which landmark case did the Supreme Court hold that the 'basic structure' of the Constitution cannot be amended?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati (1973), the Supreme Court propounded the basic structure doctrine, limiting Parliament's amending power.

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution of India gives the High Court the power to issue writs for enforcement of fundamental rights?
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs for enforcement of fundamental rights and for any other purpose.

questions.question: The concept of 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) in India originated from which case?
options: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India
Hussainara Khatoon v. Home Secy., Bihar
Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India
questions.explanation: PIL was liberalized in the judges' transfer case (S.P. Gupta, 1981), allowing any public-spirited citizen to approach courts for public causes.

questions.question: What is the tenure of a judge of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.explanation: A Supreme Court judge holds office until attaining the age of 65 years, as per Article 124(2).

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India?
options: Advisory jurisdiction on any question of law
Original jurisdiction in disputes between states
Revisory jurisdiction over High Court decisions
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has original, appellate, and advisory jurisdiction; 'revisory jurisdiction' is not a separate type—it is part of appellate jurisdiction.

questions.question: Appointment of District Judges in a state is made by the Governor in consultation with which authority?
questions.explanation: As per Article 233, appointment of district judges is made by the Governor in consultation with the High Court of the state.

questions.question: What is the primary function of a 'Lok Adalat'?
questions.explanation: Lok Adalats are statutory forums for amicable settlement of pending or pre-litigation disputes through compromise.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Constitution can the Supreme Court grant special leave to appeal against any judgment of a High Court?
questions.explanation: Article 136 empowers the Supreme Court to grant special leave to appeal from any court or tribunal in India.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued to prevent a lower court from exceeding its jurisdiction?
questions.explanation: A writ of Prohibition is issued by a higher court to a lower court to prevent it from exceeding its jurisdiction.

questions.question: The doctrine of 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution was propounded in which case?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) propounded the Basic Structure doctrine.

questions.question: In which year was the National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC) Act passed by the Parliament?
questions.explanation: The NJAC Act was passed in 2014 to replace the collegium system, but was later struck down by the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Under the Panchayati Raj system, which court has the jurisdiction to decide election disputes of Panchayat members?
questions.explanation: Election disputes of Panchayat members are decided by the Civil Court as per the Tamil Nadu Panchayats Act.

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution of India provides for the establishment of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 provides for the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court.

questions.question: The power of judicial review in India is derived from which of the following Articles?
questions.explanation: Article 13 declares laws inconsistent with fundamental rights void, thereby enabling judicial review.

questions.question: The Collegium system for appointment of judges to the Supreme Court was evolved through which landmark case?
questions.explanation: The Collegium system was established by the Supreme Court in the 1993 judgment of Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association v. Union of India.

questions.question: Which Constitutional Amendment Act curtailed the power of judicial review over constitutional amendments?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment (1976) introduced Article 368(4) and (5) to limit judicial review of constitutional amendments.

questions.question: The concept of 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution was propounded in which case?
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), the Supreme Court held that the basic structure of the Constitution cannot be amended.

questions.question: Under which Article can the President of India seek advisory opinion from the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 143 empowers the President to refer any question of law or fact to the Supreme Court for its opinion.

questions.question: The power of the High Court to issue writs for enforcement of fundamental rights is under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 226 grants High Courts the power to issue writs for enforcement of fundamental rights and for other purposes.

questions.question: Which Directive Principle in the Constitution ensures the independence of the judiciary by separating it from the executive?
questions.explanation: Article 50 directs the State to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services.

questions.question: Who was the first Law Minister of independent India and played a key role in shaping the judiciary provisions in the Constituent Assembly?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, as Chairman of the Drafting Committee and first Law Minister, was instrumental in framing the judiciary articles.

questions.question: Which of the following High Courts in India was established first?
questions.explanation: The Calcutta High Court was established on July 1, 1862, the first High Court under the Indian High Courts Act 1861.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued to secure the release of a person who has been illegally detained?
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus is a writ that orders the detaining authority to produce the person before the court to check the legality of detention.

questions.question: The doctrine of 'Basic Structure' of the Indian Constitution was propounded in which landmark case?
options: Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala (1973)
Minerva Mills vs Union of India (1980)
S.R. Bommai vs Union of India (1994)
questions.explanation: In Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), the Supreme Court ruled that Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution.

questions.question: Under which article of the Indian Constitution are the appointment of judges to the Supreme Court made by the President?
questions.explanation: Article 124 deals with the establishment and constitution of the Supreme Court, including appointment of judges by the President.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding the collegium system for judicial appointments is correct?
options: It is a constitutional body established by the 99th Amendment Act.
It was struck down by the Supreme Court in the NJAC case.
It involves the President of India as the final appointing authority.
It is based on a series of Supreme Court judgments, not a constitutional provision.
questions.explanation: The collegium system evolved through the Second, Third, and Fourth Judges Cases, and is not a statutory or constitutional creation.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Supreme Court's power of judicial review: 1. It is a part of the basic structure of the Constitution. 2. It can review any law passed by Parliament, including constitutional amendments. 3. It is explicitly mentioned in Article 13. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Judicial review is a basic structure feature (Kesavananda Bharati case). It can review constitutional amendments (basic structure test). Article 13 explicitly provides for judicial review of laws, not amendments.

questions.question: A High Court judge can be removed from office on the ground of proved misbehaviour or incapacity. Who initiates the removal process?
options: The President of India on his own satisfaction.
The Chief Justice of India on recommendation of a collegium.
The Parliament through a resolution passed by a two-thirds majority.
The Governor of the state on the advice of the state cabinet.
questions.explanation: A High Court judge can be removed only by an order of the President after an address by each House of Parliament supported by a majority of total membership and a two-thirds majority of members present and voting.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the District Judiciary in India?
options: Adjudicating civil and criminal cases at the district level.
Exercising superintendence over subordinate courts within the district.
Issuing writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights.
Hearing appeals from lower courts within the district.
questions.explanation: The power to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights is vested in the Supreme Court (Article 32) and High Courts (Article 226), not the district judiciary.

questions.question: The concept of 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) was pioneered in India to:
options: Allow any citizen to directly approach the Supreme Court for any legal dispute.
Provide legal aid to poor litigants in criminal cases.
Enable access to justice for disadvantaged groups through representative actions.
Replace traditional adversarial litigation with inquisitorial proceedings.
questions.explanation: PIL was developed by the Supreme Court in the 1980s to allow any public-spirited person to file a petition on behalf of those unable to approach the court, expanding access to justice.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the Supreme Court and High Courts under Article 141?
options: High Courts are bound by the obiter dicta of the Supreme Court.
The law declared by the Supreme Court is binding on all courts within India.
High Courts can overrule Supreme Court judgments in their respective states.
The Supreme Court's decisions are binding only on itself and the High Courts.
questions.explanation: Article 141 states that the law declared by the Supreme Court shall be binding on all courts within the territory of India.

questions.question: A law passed by the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly is challenged as violating the basic structure of the Constitution. Which court has the primary jurisdiction to hear this case?
options: Both Madras High Court and Supreme Court concurrently.
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine applies only to constitutional amendments, and the Supreme Court has the exclusive jurisdiction to determine if a constitutional amendment violates the basic structure. State laws are tested against fundamental rights, not basic structure.

questions.question: Under Article 233 of the Constitution, the appointment of district judges in a state is made by the:
options: Governor of the state in consultation with the High Court.
Chief Justice of the High Court with the approval of the President.
State Public Service Commission on merit basis.
President of India on the recommendation of the Chief Justice of India.
questions.explanation: Article 233 provides that appointments of district judges shall be made by the Governor of the state in consultation with the High Court exercising jurisdiction in relation to that state.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the 'doctrine of stare decisis' in the Indian judiciary is correct?
options: It is not applicable to the Supreme Court, which can overrule its own decisions.
It binds High Courts to follow decisions of other High Courts.
It prevents the Supreme Court from reviewing its own judgments.
It applies only to criminal cases, not civil cases.
questions.explanation: Stare decisis is generally followed, but the Supreme Court is not bound by its own previous decisions and can overrule them in the interest of justice or to correct errors.

questions.question: Consider the following: 1. Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court to hear appeals from High Courts in civil matters. 2. Power of the High Court to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights. 3. Power of the District Court to try cases under the Panchayati Raj Act. Which of the above is/are derived from the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: Supreme Court's appellate jurisdiction (Article 133) and High Court's writ jurisdiction (Article 226) are constitutional. District Court's jurisdiction under Panchayati Raj Act is statutory, not constitutional.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes the relationship between the 'basic structure' doctrine and the power of judicial review of the Supreme Court of India?
options: The basic structure doctrine is a creation of the Supreme Court to limit Parliament's amending power, and it forms the core of its judicial review power.
The basic structure doctrine is explicitly mentioned in Article 368, empowering the Court to review constitutional amendments.
The basic structure doctrine applies only to amendments affecting Fundamental Rights under Part III.
The basic structure doctrine was first propounded in the Golaknath case (1967) to uphold the supremacy of Parliament.
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine, evolved in Kesavananda Bharati (1973), limits Parliament's amending power and is the foundation of the Supreme Court's power to judicially review constitutional amendments.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the appointment of judges to the Supreme Court of India under the collegium system: 1. The Chief Justice of India (CJI) is appointed on the basis of seniority. 2. The collegium for appointment of Supreme Court judges consists of the CJI and four senior-most judges of the Supreme Court. 3. The government can reject a collegium recommendation only if it provides reasons for national security concerns. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is correct (seniority convention). Statement 2 is correct (collegium composition for SC judges). Statement 3 is incorrect; the government can seek reconsideration but cannot outright reject on national security grounds alone.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a ground for the removal of a judge of a High Court in India, as provided under the Constitution?
options: Violation of the code of conduct prescribed by the Supreme Court
questions.explanation: Removal of a High Court judge is governed by Article 217(1)(b) read with Article 124(4), which requires 'proved misbehavior' or 'incapacity'. Violation of a code of conduct is not a constitutional ground.

questions.question: Which of the following cases is most directly associated with the Supreme Court's interpretation that the 'right to life' under Article 21 includes the right to a clean environment and the doctrine of 'public trust'?
options: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak case)
Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India (CRZ case)
Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India
questions.explanation: In M.C. Mehta v. Kamal Nath (1997), the Supreme Court applied the 'public trust doctrine' to environmental protection, linking it to Article 21.

questions.question: Under the Indian Constitution, the 'doctrine of severability' is applied by the judiciary to:
options: Separate the valid parts of a law from the invalid ones, saving the law to the extent possible.
Separate the legislative powers between the Union and State lists.
Separate the fundamental rights from directive principles.
Separate the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court from the High Courts.
questions.explanation: The doctrine of severability, under Article 13, allows courts to strike down only the unconstitutional part of a law while retaining the rest if it can be separated.

questions.question: Which of the following amendments to the Indian Constitution directly curtailed the power of judicial review of the Supreme Court in the context of elections?
questions.explanation: The 39th Amendment (1975) placed the election of the Prime Minister and Speaker outside the purview of judicial review, but it was struck down in Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975).

questions.question: In the context of Panchayati Raj institutions, the power of the State Election Commission to conduct elections is subject to judicial review by which court?
options: Only the Supreme Court of India
Only the respective High Court of the state
Both the High Court and the Supreme Court
The District Court under the supervision of the High Court
questions.explanation: Actions of the State Election Commission, being a quasi-judicial body, are subject to judicial review by both the High Court (under Article 226) and the Supreme Court (under Article 32/136).

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the 'curative petition' in the Supreme Court of India is correct?
options: It is a constitutional remedy under Article 137, but can be filed only after the dismissal of a review petition.
It can be filed directly against any judgment without filing a review petition first.
It is heard by a bench of three judges, and the petitioner must prove a miscarriage of justice.
It was introduced by the 44th Amendment to the Constitution.
questions.explanation: A curative petition, evolved by the Supreme Court in Rupa Ashok Hurra (2002), is the last judicial remedy and can be filed only after a review petition is dismissed.

questions.question: The concept of 'judicial activism' in India is often associated with which of the following judicial innovations?
options: Public Interest Litigation (PIL) and the relaxation of locus standi
The power of contempt of court
The principle of natural justice (audi alteram partem)
questions.explanation: Judicial activism in India is most prominently linked to the Supreme Court's expansion of PIL, allowing any public-spirited citizen to approach the court for enforcement of rights.

questions.question: Under Article 226 of the Constitution, a High Court can issue writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights and for 'any other purpose'. Which of the following is NOT a valid ground for a High Court to issue a writ under 'any other purpose'?
options: To correct an error of law apparent on the face of the record by a lower tribunal
To enforce a statutory duty owed by a public authority
To challenge the constitutional validity of a state law on grounds of legislative competence
To settle a private contractual dispute between two individuals involving no public law element
questions.explanation: Writs under Article 226 are available only for public law remedies (enforcement of rights, statutory duties, or jurisdictional errors), not for private contractual disputes.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the collegium system for appointment of judges: 1. It was introduced by the 99th Constitutional Amendment Act. 2. The Supreme Court collegium comprises the Chief Justice of India and four senior-most judges. 3. The system was upheld as valid by the Supreme Court in the Second Judges Case (1993). Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is false; the 99th Amendment introduced the NJAC, not the collegium. Statements 2 and 3 are correct per the Second Judges Case (1993).

questions.question: With reference to the powers of the Supreme Court under Article 142, which of the following statements is correct?
options: It can override a law made by Parliament if necessary to do complete justice.
It can modify a fundamental right to ensure justice.
It can pass any decree or order, but it must not contravene any existing law.
It can grant divorce by mutual consent even if the statutory waiting period is not completed.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court held in Union Carbide case that Article 142 enables it to pass orders to do complete justice, even overriding statutory provisions, subject to constitutional limitations.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a qualification for appointment as a judge of the Supreme Court of India?
options: Must be a citizen of India.
Must have been a judge of a High Court for at least five years.
Must have been an advocate of a High Court for at least ten years.
Must have attained the age of 45 years.
questions.explanation: The Constitution does not prescribe a minimum age of 45 for Supreme Court judges; the age requirement is only for retirement (65 years).

questions.question: In the context of the Indian judiciary, the term 'curative petition' is:
options: A petition filed for the review of a judgment after the time limit for review expires.
The last remedy available to a litigant after the dismissal of a review petition.
A petition filed to correct a clerical error in a Supreme Court judgment.
A petition filed to challenge the constitutional validity of a law.
questions.explanation: A curative petition is the final judicial remedy after a review petition is dismissed, introduced by the Supreme Court in Rupa Ashok Hurra v. Ashok Hurra (2002).

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the High Courts in India: 1. The Chief Justice of a High Court is appointed by the President in consultation with the Chief Justice of India and the Governor of the state. 2. The number of judges in a High Court is fixed by the President. 3. A High Court can issue writs for enforcement of fundamental rights as well as for other purposes. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is correct per Article 217; statement 2 is false (strength is fixed by Parliament); statement 3 is correct (Article 226 allows writs for other purposes too).

questions.question: The doctrine of 'pith and substance' is primarily used by Indian courts to:
options: Interpret the extent of the executive power of the Union and States.
Resolve conflicts between Union and State laws on overlapping subjects.
Determine whether a law violates the basic structure of the Constitution.
Adjudicate on the validity of amendments to the Constitution.
questions.explanation: The doctrine of pith and substance helps determine the true nature of a law when there is a conflict over legislative competence under the Seventh Schedule.

questions.question: Which of the following judgments of the Supreme Court established the principle that the power of judicial review is part of the 'basic structure' of the Constitution?
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973)
Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980)
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994)
questions.explanation: In Minerva Mills (1980), the Supreme Court held that judicial review is a basic feature, though Kesavananda Bharati first propounded the basic structure doctrine.

questions.question: Under the Constitution of India, the power to transfer a judge from one High Court to another vests in:
options: The President on the recommendation of the Chief Justice of India.
The President on the advice of the Prime Minister.
The Chief Justice of India with the consent of the judge concerned.
The Supreme Court collegium and the President.
questions.explanation: Article 222 provides for transfer of High Court judges by the President after consultation with the Chief Justice of India; consent of the judge is not required.

questions.question: Which of the following constitutional provisions is NOT directly related to the independence of the judiciary?
options: Prohibition on the discussion of the conduct of judges of the Supreme Court in Parliament (Article 121).
Security of tenure for judges (Articles 124 and 217).
Power of Parliament to reduce the salaries and allowances of judges during their term.
Appointment of judges through a consultative process (Article 124 and 217).
questions.explanation: Article 125 ensures that salaries of judges cannot be varied to their disadvantage during their term; reducing them would undermine independence.

questions.question: Consider the following statements: 1. The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in any dispute between two or more states. 2. The Supreme Court has advisory jurisdiction on matters referred by the President under Article 143. 3. The Supreme Court's appellate jurisdiction includes appeals from High Courts in both civil and criminal matters. Which of these statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: All three statements are correct: Article 131 (original jurisdiction), Article 143 (advisory), and Articles 132-136 (appellate jurisdiction).

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the 'basic structure' doctrine of the Indian Constitution?
options: It was first propounded in the Golaknath case (1967).
It was first propounded in the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973).
It was first propounded in the Minerva Mills case (1980).
It was first propounded in the Shankari Prasad case (1951).
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine was established by the Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), holding that Parliament cannot amend the basic features of the Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements: 1. The Supreme Court can transfer a case from one High Court to another. 2. The Supreme Court can transfer a case from a subordinate court to itself. Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Under Article 139A, the Supreme Court can transfer cases between High Courts and also withdraw cases from subordinate courts to itself.

questions.question: The power of the Supreme Court to review its own judgment is derived from which of the following?
questions.explanation: Article 137 empowers the Supreme Court to review its own judgments or orders, subject to laws made by Parliament.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a condition for the appointment of a judge to the Supreme Court of India?
options: Must be a citizen of India
Must have been a judge of a High Court for at least 5 years
Must have been an advocate of a High Court for at least 10 years
Must have attained the age of 65 years
questions.explanation: The retirement age for a Supreme Court judge is 65 years, but it is not a condition for appointment; it is a condition for tenure.

questions.question: In the context of the Collegium system for judicial appointments, which of the following statements is correct?
options: It was introduced by the 99th Constitutional Amendment Act.
It was struck down by the Supreme Court in the NJAC case (2015).
It gives the executive the final say in appointments.
It was first introduced in the First Judges Case (1981).
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court struck down the NJAC Act and the 99th Amendment in 2015, reinstating the Collegium system for judicial appointments.

questions.question: Which of the following writs is issued to a public authority to perform a duty that it has failed to perform?
questions.explanation: Mandamus is a writ commanding a public authority to perform a statutory duty it has unlawfully neglected or refused to perform.

questions.question: Which of the following cases established the principle that the Preamble is a part of the Constitution?
